entry.title: Combined summary - Major BIP 360 Update
entry.summary: In an effort to address community feedback and enhance the clarity of BIP 360 documentation, Ethan Heilman, Isabel Foxen Duke, and Hunter Beast have embarked on a comprehensive rewrite. This revision was deemed necessary following previous updates that introduced significant technical changes, highlighting the need for a fresh approach to improve the proposal's clarity and effectively articulate strategies for mitigating quantum-related risks. The revised BIP 360 maintains its stance against incorporating post-quantum signature schemes but introduces a new output type known as "Pay-to-Tapscript-Hash (P2TSH)." This addition aims to omit the key path spend, thereby protecting against potential vulnerabilities in Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) posed by advancements in quantum computing or other cryptographic technologies, marking a pivotal step towards enhancing Bitcoin's quantum resistance. The inclusion of test vectors in Python and Rust within the updated proposal facilitates a broader understanding and easier implementation of the proposed changes, inviting community review and collaboration through platforms such as the [BIP Repo](https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/pull/1670) and [BIP360.org](http://bip360.org). This initiative underscores a commitment to refining Bitcoin's security measures in response to evolving cryptanalytic technologies. However, concerns have been raised regarding the future compatibility and clarity of the term Pay-to-Tapscript-Hash (P2TSH), especially with the possibility of new tapleaf versions incorporating Simplicity—a high-level programming language designed for blockchain applications. The naming convention is crucial for clear communication among developers, users, and stakeholders, suggesting the need for terminology that remains intuitive and accurately descriptive as blockchain technology evolves. The dialogue extends to exploring alternative transaction verification methods aimed at safeguarding against quantum computing threats, specifically through a multi-step secret reveal mechanism utilizing TXHASH. This approach emphasizes script-only transactions and incorporates operations like OP_CTV and OP_TXHASH to achieve quantum resistance, with further details available at [Delving into Bitcoin](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/a-quantum-resistance-script-only-using-op-ctv-op-txhash-and-no-new-signatures/2168). Additionally, the discussion highlights the importance of terminology precision, particularly concerning the misleading use of "hash" in the context of script trees and payments directed towards a script tree's root. This conversation underlines the necessity for clear and precise language in cryptography to convey critical system attributes, such as quantum-resistant properties effectively. Amidst these technical deliberations, there's an ongoing debate about the appropriateness of the BIP-360 name, given its similarity to BIP-341 but without taproot, which could mislead stakeholders about its functionality. The critique suggests renaming the proposal to more accurately reflect its function, highlighting the importance of precision and clarity in the development and naming of technical proposals within the cryptocurrency domain. Furthermore, considerations for adopting "pay to script tree hash" or P2STH as a preferred term are discussed, emphasizing the need for descriptive naming conventions that accurately represent the address class's functionality. This debate illustrates the broader challenge of developing a lexicon that is both forward-looking and reflective of the evolving landscape of blockchain technology.
